'''Musa no Kuni no Miyatsuko''' (''musa no kuni no miyatsuko'', ''musa kokuzō'') was a {{ill|kuni no miyatsuko|ja|国造}} who ruled {{ill|Musha District|ja|武射郡}} of later {{ill|Kazusa Province|ja|上総国}} (a {{ill|ritsuryō province|ja|令制国}}), corresponding to present-day {{ill|Sanmu, Chiba|ja|山武市|lt=Sanmu City|lt_ja=山武市}} and the vicinity of {{ill|Shibayama, Chiba|ja|芝山町|lt=Shibayama Town|lt_ja=芝山町}} and {{ill|Yokoshibahikari, Chiba|ja|横芝光町|lt=Yokoshibahikari Town|lt_ja=横芝光町}} in {{ill|Sanmu District, Chiba|ja|山武郡|lt=Sanmu District|lt_ja=山武郡}}, {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|ja|千葉県}}<ref group="Note">The east bank of the {{ill|Kuriyama River|ja|栗山川}} in Yokoshibahikari Town is considered the territory of the Shimotsu-Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko, or alternatively, the {{ill|Ogawadai Kofun Group|ja|小川台古墳群}} is considered the {{ill|okutsuki|ja|奥都城}} of the Mononobe {{ill|Sōsa clan|ja|匝瑳氏}}, and the area is considered the territory of {{ill|Sōsa District|ja|匝瑳郡}} established by {{ill|Mononobe no Ōji|ja|物部小事}}.</ref>.

== Explanation ==
Musa is also written as Musha or Muja. In the ''{{ill|Kojiki|ja|古事記}}'' section of {{ill|Emperor Kōshō|ja|孝昭天皇}}, {{ill|Ame-Tarashi-Hiko-Kuni-Oshi-Hito-no-Mikoto|ja|天足彦国押人命}}, the first prince of Emperor Kōshō, is made the ancestor of the Muja-no-Omi, and in the "Kokuzō Hongi" of the ''{{ill|Sendai Kuji Hongi|ja|先代旧事本紀}}'', during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Seimu|ja|成務天皇}}, Hiko-Oshihito-no-Mikoto, grandson of Hiko-Oketsu-no-Mikoto, ancestor of the {{ill|Wani clan|ja|和珥氏|lt=Wani clan|lt_ja=和邇氏}}, was appointed Musa no Kuni no Miyatsuko. Additionally, the Wani clan genealogy records Hiko-Oshihito-no-Mikoto as Musa no Kuni no Miyatsuko, making the Wani clan the ancestor of the Musa no Kuni no Miyatsuko and the {{ill|Kasuga clan|ja|春日氏}}<ref name="続"/><ref group="Note">In the Wani clan genealogy, the {{ill|Nukata no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|額田国造}} and {{ill|Kibi-Anakuni no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|吉備穴国造}} are also considered of the same ancestry.</ref>; claiming common ancestry with the Wani clan and holding the {{ill|kabane|ja|カバネ|lt=kabane|lt_ja=カバネ}} of {{ill|Omi|ja|臣}}, there are no other examples among kuni no miyatsuko of the {{ill|Tōgoku|ja|東国|lt=Tōgoku|lt_ja=東国}}<ref name="日">''Nihon Kodai-shi Chimei Jiten'', p. 238</ref>.

The later Musha District, which was the territory, extended northward as the northern tip of Kazusa Province to such an extent that it seems like part of {{ill|Shimōsa Province|ja|下総国}}. Nevertheless, it being incorporated into a corner of Kazusa Province is said to have its own {{ill|historical|ja|歴史|lt=historical|lt_ja=歴史}} and {{ill|geographical|ja|地理|lt=geographical|lt_ja=地理}} reasons<ref name="日"/>, with one theory being that {{ill|reclamation|ja|開拓|lt=reclamation|lt_ja=開拓}} proceeded from the {{ill|Pacific|ja|太平洋}} side via the {{ill|Kuroshio Current|ja|黒潮}}<ref>''Chiba-ken no Rekishi'', p. 3</ref>.

In the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, in the ''{{ill|Shoku Nihongi|ja|続日本紀}}'' entry for the 13th day of the 3rd month of {{ill|Jingo-keiun|ja|神護景雲}} 3 ([[769]]), the name of Kasukabe-no-Okumaro of {{ill|Oshika District|ja|牡鹿郡}}, {{ill|Mutsu Province|ja|陸奥国}}, who was granted the title Musha-no-Omi, appears, and he is considered a figure connected to {{ill|Ijimi Miyake|ja|伊甚屯倉}} and this kuni no miyatsuko<ref name="続">''Shoku Nihongi 4'', pp. 233, 529</ref>.

== Development of the Kuni no Miyatsuko System ==
=== Up to the early 6th century ===
In Chiba Prefecture, where the greatest number of {{ill|keyhole-shaped kofun|ja|前方後円墳}} in all of Japan's prefectures have been confirmed<ref group="Note">According to tabulations in ''Zenpō-kōenfun Shūsei'', 677 keyhole-shaped kofun, the most in Japan, have been confirmed.</ref>, despite the Musa no Kuni no Miyatsuko holding the highest-rank kabane of Omi in the {{ill|Yamato kingship|ja|ヤマト王権}}, no conspicuous kofun were constructed in their territory until the early {{ill|6th century|ja|6世紀|lt=6th century|lt_ja=6世紀}}. However, afterward in the latter half of the 6th century, the construction of large-scale kofun began, and many kofun came to be constructed rapidly. Also, since the territory of this kuni no miyatsuko is situated between the territories of the {{ill|Kami-tsu-Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|上海上国造}} and the {{ill|Shimotsu-Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|下海上国造}}, there is considered to be at least two stages in the development of the kuni no miyatsuko system in {{ill|Bōsō|ja|房総}}. There is also a view that until the {{ill|5th century|ja|5世紀|lt=5th century|lt_ja=5世紀}} there existed a sphere of influence that could be called "Greater Unakami Province," ruling the area spanning from central Chiba Prefecture to {{ill|Ibaraki Prefecture|ja|茨城県}}, {{ill|Saitama Prefecture|ja|埼玉県}}, and {{ill|Tokyo|ja|東京都|lt=Tokyo|lt_ja=東京都}}, but in the 6th century this kuni no miyatsuko — a clan of the Wani clan, a powerful {{ill|maternal relative|ja|外戚}} of the {{ill|great king|ja|治天下大王|lt=great king|lt_ja=大王}} of the {{ill|Kinai|ja|畿内}} — advanced into the area, and Greater Unakami Province was divided into the Kami-tsu-Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko and Shimotsu-Unakami no Kuni no Miyatsuko and declined<ref>''Kodai Tōgoku no Fūkei'', pp. 18-21</ref><ref group="Note">The ''Teiō Hennenki'' dates the establishment of Kazusa Province to the 1st year ([[534]]) of {{ill|Emperor Ankan|ja|安閑天皇}}, and there is also a theory that views the division of Kazusa Province and Shimōsa Province as occurring in the mid-6th century at the same time.</ref><ref group="Note">The ''Nihon Shoki'' entry for the 4th month of the 1st year (534) of Emperor Ankan contains an article stating that the Ijimi Miyake was established for the sake of {{ill|Empress Kasuga|ja|春日山田皇女|lt=Empress Kasuga|lt_ja=春日皇后}}, who had a mother of the Wani clan, said to be of the same clan.</ref>.

=== Heyday of kofun construction ===
Kofun of Musa Province, whose full-scale construction began in the latter half of the 6th century, are said to have high academic value along with their great number, including the {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Group|ja|芝山古墳群}}, a rare example where rows of {{ill|haniwa|ja|埴輪}} were preserved intact in their original position, the {{ill|Kabunoki Kofun Group|ja|蕪木古墳群}}, which yielded gilt-bronze sword-sheaths and gilt-bronze purse-shaped vessels unseen elsewhere, and the {{ill|Ōzutsumi-Gongenzuka Kofun|ja|大堤権現塚古墳}}, with triple surrounding trenches and said to be the largest {{ill|end-of-Kofun-period kofun|ja|古墳時代#古墳時代終末期|lt=end-of-Kofun-period kofun|lt_ja=終末期の古墳}} in Japan, among other outstanding keyhole-shaped kofun. Since short-term construction is unlikely, the {{ill|common view|ja|通説}} was that keyhole-shaped kofun continued to be constructed into the {{ill|7th century|ja|7世紀}} even after their end in the Kinai.

=== End of keyhole-shaped kofun ===
Not limited to this area, in the {{ill|Kantō region|ja|関東地方}} from the 6th to 7th century, kofun surpassing those of the Kinai were built, and regarding {{ill|square kofun|ja|方墳}} and {{ill|round kofun|ja|円墳}} after the end of keyhole-shaped kofun, some surpassed the tombs of the great kings; their details were not necessarily clarified. Among these, the {{ill|Danotsuka Kofun|ja|駄ノ塚古墳}}, considered a large square terminal-period kofun within the territory of this kuni no miyatsuko, was particularly valued, and {{ill|excavation|ja|発掘調査|lt=excavation|lt_ja=発掘調査}} was carried out by the {{ill|National Museum of Japanese History|ja|国立歴史民俗博物館}} from [[1985]]. One of the achievements of this excavation was that the construction period could be identified to the [[610]]s.

The Danotsuka Kofun is one of the kofun making up the Itazuke Kofun Group built on the upper east bank of the {{ill|Sakuta River|ja|作田川}} (Naruto River), which flows into {{ill|Kujūkuri Beach|ja|九十九里浜}}; it was constructed after the Nishinodai Kofun and the Fudōzuka Kofun, and after it, Danotsuka-nishi Kofun was constructed. To the northeast, on the east bank of the {{ill|Sakai River (Sanmu)|ja|境川 (山武市)|lt=Sakai River|lt_ja=境川}}, is the Gomategadai Kofun Group including the {{ill|Gomategadai Kofun No. 16|ja|胡麻手台16号墳}}, and further east, on the middle east bank of the {{ill|Kido River (Chiba)|ja|木戸川 (千葉県)|lt=Kido River|lt_ja=木戸川}}, are the {{ill|Ōzutsumi|ja|大堤権現塚古墳|lt=Ōzutsumi|lt_ja=大堤}} and {{ill|Kabunoki Kofun Group|ja|蕪木古墳群}}, and on the upper east bank, the {{ill|Koike Ōtsuka Kofun|ja|小池大塚古墳|lt=Koike|lt_ja=小池}} and {{ill|Shibayama Kofun Group|ja|芝山古墳群}}, with many late- to terminal-period keyhole-shaped kofun existing in this area. With the construction period of the Danotsuka Kofun, a square kofun after the keyhole-shaped kofun, having been identified, the construction periods of these kofun have been reconsidered, and it is now thought (though some are still dated to the early 7th century) that with the end of the 6th century, the construction of keyhole-shaped kofun throughout all of Japan was halted simultaneously.

Additionally, the four kofun groups of the Itazuke, Gomategadai, Ōzutsumi-Kabunoki, and Koike-Shibayama are considered to have been constructed concurrently, and during this period four powers that constructed large keyhole-shaped kofun existed in this area, converging into one power at the beginning of the 7th century when the Danotsuka Kofun was constructed<ref group="Note">In the middle reaches of the Kido River, between the Koike-Shibayama Kofun Group and the Ōzutsumi-Kabunoki Kofun Group, is the {{ill|Yamamuro-himezuka Kofun|ja|山室姫塚古墳}}, which is considered a large terminal-period round kofun, so it is conceivable that the position of the Musa no Kuni no Miyatsuko temporarily shifted to the power in the Kido River basin to the east.</ref>, moving toward the establishment of the {{ill|ritsuryō system|ja|律令制#日本の律令制|lt=ritsuryō system|lt_ja=律令制}}<ref group="Note">Downstream of the Gomategadai Kofun Group on the east bank of the Sakai River are the Shimatohigashi site, considered to be the remains of Musha District government office, and the Shingyōji Ruined Temple, considered to be the earliest temple founded in Bōsō.</ref> from the kuni no miyatsuko system. This is considered to apply not only to this kuni no miyatsuko but also similarly to the {{ill|Suwe no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|須恵国造}}, {{ill|Umakuta no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|馬来田国造}}, {{ill|Inba no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|印波国造}}, and {{ill|Kamitsukenu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|上毛野国造}}, which constructed similar terminal-period large square kofun<ref group="Note">At this same time, in the territories of the Suwe no Kuni no Miyatsuko, Umakuta no Kuni no Miyatsuko, Inba no Kuni no Miyatsuko, and Kamitsukenu no Kuni no Miyatsuko, the large square kofun {{ill|Warimizuka Kofun|ja|割見塚古墳}}, {{ill|Matsuzura Kofun|ja|松面古墳}}, {{ill|Ryūkakuji Iwaya Kofun|ja|龍角寺岩屋古墳}}, and {{ill|Sōja Atago-yama Kofun|ja|総社愛宕山古墳}} respectively were constructed.</ref><ref group="Note">At this same time, in the territory of the Muzashi no Kuni no Miyatsuko, the large round kofun {{ill|Hachimanyama Kofun (Gyōda)|ja|八幡山古墳 (行田市)|lt=Hachimanyama Kofun|lt_ja=八幡山古墳}} was constructed, and in the territory of the Shimotsukenu no Kuni no Miyatsuko, the large round kofun {{ill|Kurumazuka Kofun (Mibu)|ja|車塚古墳 (壬生町)|lt=Mibu-Kurumazuka Kofun|lt_ja=壬生車塚古墳}} was constructed.</ref>, and also to the {{ill|Muzashi no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|无邪志国造}} and {{ill|Shimotsukenu no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|下毛野国造}}, which constructed large round kofun though of different shape<ref>''Tōgoku no Kofun to Kodaishi'', pp. 152-158</ref>.

== Footnotes ==
{{脚注ヘルプ}}
=== Notes ===
{{Reflist|group="Note"}}
=== Citations ===
{{Reflist|30em}}

== References ==
* Aoki Kazuo et al., ''Shoku Nihongi 4'', {{ill|Iwanami Shoten|ja|岩波書店}}, 1995, ISBN 4-00-240015-8
* Katō Kenkichi et al., ''Nihon Kodai-shi Chimei Jiten'', {{ill|Yūzankaku|ja|雄山閣}}, 2007, ISBN 978-4-639-01995-4
* Ishii Susumu et al., ''Chiba-ken no Rekishi'', {{ill|Yamakawa Shuppansha|ja|山川出版社}}, 2000, ISBN 4-634-32120-3
* Harashima Reiji, ''Kodai Tōgoku no Fūkei'', {{ill|Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ja|吉川弘文館}}, 1993, ISBN 4-642-07394-9
* Shiraishi Taichirō, ''Tōgoku no Kofun to Kodaishi'', {{ill|Gakuseisha|ja|学生社}}, 2007, ISBN 978-4-311-20298-8
* Kondō Yoshirō et al., ''Zenpō-kōenfun Shūsei'', Yamakawa Shuppansha, 1994, ISBN 4-634-50010-8

== External links ==
* [https://www.haniwakan.com/kenkyu/boso/musa.html Walking the Bōsō Kofun: Kofun of Musa Province]

{{Japanese-history-stub}}

{{DEFAULTSORT:むさのくにのみやつこ}}
[[Category:上総国]]
[[Category:千葉県の歴史]]
[[Category:和珥氏|*]]
[[Category:Kuni no miyatsuko]]
[[ja:武社国造]]
[[Category:Need translation]]
